chunk_summary: Guidelines for safe tapering, cycling, and transitioning from parenteral to enteral nutrition, focusing on hypoglycemia prevention, glucose monitoring, and stepwise PN reduction strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the risks of hypoglycemia during PN interruption and strategies to prevent it.
Learn how to implement cycling PN in stable patients and monitor glucose levels effectively.
Master the stepwise approach to transitioning from PN to enteral nutrition, including stop rules.
Recognize the importance of tracking total daily intake from all nutritional sources.
key_facts: Abrupt PN cessation can cause hypoglycemia, especially in infants and small children.
Taper PN over 1–2 hours or use 10% dextrose-containing maintenance fluids to prevent hypoglycemia.
Cycling PN (12–18 hours/day) is useful for stable patients with cholestasis or preparing for home PN.
Transition to enteral feeding involves halving PN calories when EN reaches ~50% of goal.
Discontinue PN when EN provides 66–75% of needs for 24–48 hours with stable electrolytes.
Prompt alternative dextrose infusion is critical during unexpected PN interruptions.
reference_quote: Prevention starts with prescribing details: avoid abrupt cessation in infants and small children, and write a taper strategy.
document_key_topics: Stepwise transition from PN to EN
